coredata.dc:title: Enhanced etching terminal wastewater treatment and H2 production by in-situ deposited heavy metals on carbon dots/g-C3N4 photocathode microbial electrolysis cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jiahua
Wang, Qiang
Wan, Huilin
Shi, Yong
Huang, Liping
coredata.dc:description: Sustainable and cost-effective semiconducting photocathodes of microbial electrolysis cells (MECs) are attractively promising for efficient treatment of actual industrial wastewaters containing complex recalcitrant organics and multiple heavy metals. Herein carbon dots/graphitic carbon nitride (CDs/g-C3N4) photocathodes were employed to achieve efficient treatment of actual etching terminal wastewater (ETW) with simultaneous H2 production in MECs, allowing the effluent meeting national discharge standards (GB39731–2020). The progressively in-situ deposited heavy metals on the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes, formed as metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 after simple calcinations, further enhanced the ETW treatment (recalcitrant organics mineralization: 42.2 mg/L/h vs. 35.5 mg/L/h; heavy metal removal: Cu(II): 9.9 mg/L/h vs. 7.4 mg/L/h, Ni(II): 4.7 mg/L/h vs. 3.5 mg/L/h, Zn(II): 0.7 mg/L/h vs. 0.5 mg/L/h) and H2 production (0.1138 m3/m3/d vs. 0.0662 m3/m3/d). The importation of heavy metals, formed as metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 altered the proportion of reactive oxidative species and thus promoted mineralization of recalcitrant organics, besides offering additional electrochemical removal of heavy metals with simultaneous more H2 production. This study demonstrates a new feasible protocol for achieving efficient ETW treatment, and gives a comprehensive appreciation of the effect of in-situ deposited heavy metals on the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes, which has a profound effect on subsequent ETW treatment with simultaneous H2 production.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recalcitrant organics mineralization and heavy metals removal (A and D), hydrogen production (B and G), and circuital current (C and H) in the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes of MECs either as a function of number of operation cycle (A − C), or in the controls of graphite felt, g-C3N4, or under darkness or open circuital conditions (OCCs) (D, G and H). Time course of recalcitrant organics mineralization and heavy metals removal in the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes of MECs (E) or in the g-C3N4 controls (F).

Heavy metal removal and recalcitrant organics mineralization (A), hydrogen production (B), and transient photocurrent responses (C) as a function of number of cycles of the CDs/g-C3N4 cathodes. Comparison of heavy metal removal and recalcitrant organics mineralization (D), hydrogen production (E), circuital current (F), CV tests (J), and Nyquist plots of EIS spectra (K) of the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 (5th cycle), and the controls of CDs/g-C3N4, g-C3N4 or graphite felt, or under no light or open circuital (OCCs) conditions (operation time: 4 h). Time course of heavy metal removal and recalcitrant organics mineralization in the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 (5th cycle) (G), and in the controls of CDs/g-C3N4 (H) or g-C3N4 (I).

Comparison of UV–vis DRS (A), Mott-Schottky plots (B, C and D), photoluminescence spectrum (E), and transient photocurrent responses (F) of metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4, CDs/g-C3N4, g-C3N4 or metal oxides only.

Naked eye observation (A, D and G), SEM micrographs (B, E and H), and EDS spectra (C, F and I) of the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 graphite felt (A – C), the sole CDs/g-C3N4 graphite felt (D − F), and the only metal oxides/graphite felt (G − I), obtained after a 5-cycle operation in the ETW catholyte, and followed by simple calcinations. EDS elemental mapping images of C (J), N (K), O (L), Cu (M), Ni (N) and Zn (O) for the obtained metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 graphite felt.
HRTEM images of metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 (A and B), g-C3N4 (C and D), and carbon quantum dots (E and F) at scales of either 50 nm (A, C and E) or 5 nm (B, D and F).

The high-resolution XPS spectra of metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 (A – F), and controls of the CDs/g-C3N4 (A and B) or the metal oxides (C – F) associated with C 1 s (A), N 1 s (B), Cu 2p (C), Ni 2p (D), Zn 2p (E), and O 1 s (F).

Comparison of recalcitrant organics mineralization in the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 cathodes of the photo-assisted MECs fed with etching terminal wastewater under different trapping agent conditions (A), or in the controls of the sole CDs/g-C3N4 cathodes (B), or in the presence of either individual circuital current (C) or light irradiation only (D). Possible pathways of heavy metals removal and recalcitrant organic mineralization along with transformation of chemical active species in the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes (HM: heavy metals; MO: metal oxides; RO: recalcitrant organics) (E).

Heavy metal removal and recalcitrant organics mineralization (A), hydrogen production (B) and circuital current (C) as a function of number of cycles. Naked eye observation (D), SEM micrograph (E), EDS spectra (F), EDS element C (G), N (H), O (I), Cu (J), Ni (K), and Zn (L) mapping images on the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 at the end of a 10-cycle operation.

head.ce:title: Enhanced etching terminal wastewater treatment and H2 production by in-situ deposited heavy metals on carbon dots/g-C3N4 photocathode microbial electrolysis cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sustainable and cost-effective semiconducting photocathodes of microbial electrolysis cells (MECs) are attractively promising for efficient treatment of actual industrial wastewaters containing complex recalcitrant organics and multiple heavy metals. Herein carbon dots/graphitic carbon nitride (CDs/g-C3N4) photocathodes were employed to achieve efficient treatment of actual etching terminal wastewater (ETW) with simultaneous H2 production in MECs, allowing the effluent meeting national discharge standards (GB39731–2020). The progressively in-situ deposited heavy metals on the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes, formed as metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 after simple calcinations, further enhanced the ETW treatment (recalcitrant organics mineralization: 42.2 mg/L/h vs. 35.5 mg/L/h; heavy metal removal: Cu(II): 9.9 mg/L/h vs. 7.4 mg/L/h, Ni(II): 4.7 mg/L/h vs. 3.5 mg/L/h, Zn(II): 0.7 mg/L/h vs. 0.5 mg/L/h) and H2 production (0.1138 m3/m3/d vs. 0.0662 m3/m3/d). The importation of heavy metals, formed as metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 altered the proportion of reactive oxidative species and thus promoted mineralization of recalcitrant organics, besides offering additional electrochemical removal of heavy metals with simultaneous more H2 production. This study demonstrates a new feasible protocol for achieving efficient ETW treatment, and gives a comprehensive appreciation of the effect of in-situ deposited heavy metals on the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes, which has a profound effect on subsequent ETW treatment with simultaneous H2 production.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Simultaneous recalcitrant organics mineralization and H2 production

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductive photocathodes of microbial electrolysis cells (MECs) have recently emerged as a promising solar-energy conversion alternative for efficient organics degradation or H2 generation with simultaneous microbial/exoelectrogenic utilization/extraction of electrons from organic wastes at the anodes [4,32,35,42,45]. Many heavy metal-based semiconductive materials have been proven to possess efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and were complicatedly prepared as cathodes of these MECs for individual H2 production, inorganic carbon reduction, removal of single heavy metals or degradation of some single recalcitrant organics (Table S1). Nevertheless, the high cost, the process complexity and the likely risks of secondary pollution in case of release of metal ions are questionable, in addition to the less efficient removal of single organics or heavy metals, all of which largely limited its applicable treatment for complex actual wastewaters with multiple components, as recently vigorously advocated by Izadi and Yu [17], and Xu et al. [44]. It is thus highly desirable to explore environmentally friendly heavy metal-free photocathode MECs for actual complex wastewater treatment, which would move the multiple functional photo-assisted MECs closer to applicable actual wastewater treatment and thus advance this technology to the next generation.

Heterogeneous nanocomposites of carbon dots and graphitic carbon nitride (CDs/g-C3N4) where the CDs serve as both electron acceptors and donors to improve the separation and transformation of the electron-hole pairs produced on the g-C3N4, in addition to boosting the light absorbance, have recently gained tremendous attention as a promising candidate for environmental and energy-concerned photocatalytic applications due to broad spectrum absorption, appropriate electronic band structures, rapid carrier mobility, excellent chemical stability, facile synthesis methods, environmental sustainability and low cost [1,24,40,46]. Despite plenty of state-of-the-art achievements of CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalysis for individual organics degradation or sole H2 production [1], the applicable CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes of MECs for treatment of actual wastewaters e.g., etching terminal wastewater (ETW) is still scarcely reported. The ETW, effluent of the etching wastewater after passing through multiple steps treatment (e.g., solvent extraction, and/or chemical precipitation), ubiquitously contains low concentrations of multiple contaminants (e.g., Cu(II), Ni(II), Zn(II), triethanolamine oleic soap, dodecylbenzenesulfonate, tetramethyl ammonium hydroxide) [2,48]. This previously regarded unworthy and still poisonous ETW has been recently treated in sole MECs or WO3/MoO3/g-C3N4 photocathodes with simultaneous H2 production [14–16,30]. However, the costly metal semiconductors (e.g., W and Mo) and their environmental risk along with low treatment efficiencies (organics mineralization: 1.0 – 36.0 mg/L/h; heavy metals removal: 0.02 – 3.60 mg/L/h) and low H2 production (0.009 – 0.046 m3/m3/d), worthwhile appealed exploration of the sustainable and cost-effective CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode MECs for the ETW treatment.

One important aspect that affects the activity of CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes for the ETW treatment is its chemical stability over operation time. The prolonged operation of CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes for the ETW treatment reasonably results in progressively deposited heavy metals on the surface of the photocathodes, and as a result, the interaction of the cathode material with the deposited heavy metals becomes crucial for determining the long-term performance of the photocathode MECs. The implantation of metal oxides (e.g., ZnO, etc.) into CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes via simply calcinations provides a novel composite material on account of the modified energy band in favor of separation speed of photoexcited electron-hole pairs and the broadened absorption spectrum in visible light, and thus makes the multielement CDs/g-C3N4-based photocatalysts extraordinary for efficient degradation of single organics or more H2 production in conventional photocatalysis processes [1,19,40,41,43]. The CDs can thus act as an electron mediator to transport the photoactive electrons from g-C3N4 to metal oxides, then to the adsorbed contaminants, all of which together greatly promoted the degradation of individual organic contaminants [1,47]. In addition, the in-situ deposited heavy metals formed as metal oxides after simple calcinations can also act as redox mediators offering additional electron highway and thus boost electronic conductivity in electrochemical processes [20,39,40]. Therefore, the sustainable and excellent CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes along with the in-situ deposited heavy metals from actual ETW over time may alter the photoelectrochemical behaviors of the cathodes and thus the enhanced ETW treatment with concomitant more H2 production. This effect has not been systematically investigated in literature, as similarly vigorously advocated by Zuo et al. [49].

Under this background, the sustainable and cost-effective CDs/g-C3N4 reported in conventional photocatalysis processes for degradation of many single organics and/or H2 production [1,24,40,41], was employed as photocathodes of MECs for efficient actual ETW treatment in multiple batch cycles operation. The progressively in-situ deposited heavy metals, formed as metal oxides after simple calcinations, interacted with the CDs/g-C3N4, and further improved ETW treatment with concomitant more H2 production via altering proportions of reactive oxidative species (e.g., O2 -, ˙OH, holes and H2O2), and offering additional electrochemical pathways. Overall, this study makes significant progress on the applicable CDs/g-C3N4 semiconducting photocathode MECs for the efficient treatment of actual industrial wastewaters such as ETW, and gives a comprehensive appreciation of the effect of in-situ deposited heavy metals on the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes, which has a profound effect on subsequent system performance for ETW treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs/g-C3N4 synthesis and photocathodes preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of g-C3N4 and CDs/g-C3N4 was referred to the report by Liu et al. [24] and described in detail in Supporting Information (SI).

Dual-chamber MEC reactors with each a same working volume of 26 mL [35] were used in this study. The above prepared CDs/g-C3N4 electrode was employed as cathode with a saturated calomel electrode (SCE, 0.24 V versus standard hydrogen electrode, SHE) as reference electrode. Graphite felt anode was inoculated with suspended bacteria from acetate-fed microbial fuel cells and fed by nutrient solution with equivalent volume [34,35]. Initial anolyte pH was scheduled at ∼3.3 as a compromise similar to the catholyte ETW (1.98), leading to negligible potential differences between the dual chambers separated by proton exchange membrane (Membranes International, Glen Rock, NJ) [26]. All electrode potentials were reported versus SHE unless otherwise mentioned. The cathode chamber was fed with actual ETW collected from a tank in TOTO Ltd. (Dalian, China), and dominantly containing recalcitrant organics (SCOD: 206.4 ± 8.0 mg/L), Cu(II) (30.8 ± 0.1 mg/L), Ni(II) (14.6 ± 0.1 mg/L), Zn(II) (3.2 ± 0.3 mg/L), nitrogen (NH4 +-N: 0.0 ± 0.0 mg/L, NO3 --N: 2.1 ± 0.1 mg/L; NO2 --N: 0.0 ± 0.0 mg/L) and sulfur (sulfide: 0.9 ± 0.1 mg/L, SO4 2-: 7.4 ± 0.2 mg/L) with a pH of 1.98 ± 0.08 and a solution conductivity of 3.05 ± 0.00 mS/cm. Considering China wastewater discharge standards (NH4 +-N < 10 mg/L, NO3 --N < 30 mg/L, sulfide < 1.0 mg/L, none available threshold values for SO4 2-), the removal of Cu(II), Ni(II) and Zn(II) as well as mineralization of recalcitrant organics in this ETW became the emphasis in this study. An external voltage of 0.8 V was applied throughout the experiments. Tinfoil was wrapped outside anodes to exclude any light effects whereas the cathode chambers were irradiated by a 100 W iodine tungsten lamp with a light intensity of 26.9 kLux and an incident photon irradiance of 23.3 mW/cm2 [36,37]. The reactors were operated at a temperature of 25 ± 3 ℃ and all the operation was triplicate at least.

To assess the contribution of different reactive species in the mineralization of recalcitrant organics in the ETW, p-benzoquinone (0.5 – 2.0 mM), isopropanol (0.5 − 10.0 mM), ammonium oxalate (0.1 – 1.0 mM), and Fe(II)-EDTA (0.1 – 0.2 mM) were used to selectively quench O2 ·-,·OH, holes and H2O2, respectively [21,28,30], achieving the optimum dosages of p-benzoquinone (0.5 mM), isopropanol (5.0 mM), ammonium oxalate (0.5 mM) and Fe(II)-EDTA (0.1 mM) in this study.

A total of 10 fed-batch cycles were operated, during which the ETW catholyte was refreshed at the end of each cycle operation of 4 h. H2 production, removal of heavy metals (Cu(II), Ni(II) and Zn(II)), and recalcitrant organics mineralization were then assessed.

The cathodes with progressively in-situ deposited heavy metals at the end of the 1st, the 3rd, the 5th and the 7th cycles operation, were disassembled from the reactors and calcinated at muffle furnace under 300 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 2.5 ℃/min [24]. These photocathodes termed as metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 were then assembled back to the reactors for system performance. A total of 10 fed-batch cycles were operated for stability assessment.

Under conditions of CDs/g-C3N4 graphite felt cathodes, control experiments under open circuital conditions (OCCs) were operated to examine the roles of single circuital current in system performance whereas those operated under darkness conditions reflected the roles of single light irradiation in system performance. The g-C3N4 graphite felt cathodes reflected the roles of CDs in system performance whereas the graphite felt cathodes implied the system performance in the absence of both CDs and g-C3N4.

With regards to the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes, a series of control experiments including sole CDs/g-C3N4 cathodes, OCCs, no light, g-C3N4, and individual graphite felt were operated to demonstrate the roles of metal oxides, circuital current, light irradiation, both metal oxides and CDs, and tricomponent of metal oxides, CDs and g-C3N4, respectively in system performance.

Hydrogen in the cathodic headspaces of MECs was measured using a gas chromatograph (GC7900, Tianmei, Shanghai) and a molecular sieve column (TDX-01, 60 – 80, 4 mm × 2 m) with argon as the carrier gas [6,35]. The recalcitrant organics in the ETW dominantly belong to 2-naphthalenesulfonic acid formaldehyde polymer sodium salt and triethanolamine oleic soap based on a high performance liquid chromatograph with an APCI (−) ion trap mass spectrometer (Agilent HPLC – MS 6410) [14,30]. For convenient and accurate consideration, measurement of dissolved COD using manganese method was effective and was thus chosen to assess mineralization of recalcitrant organics in the effluents [14,30].

Both CV and EIS were performed using the same potentiostat (VSP, BioLogic, France) in the electrolyte of the ETW with three electrode system comprising a working electrode (e.g., cathode electrodes), a Pt foil (2 × 4 cm) counter electrode, and a saturated calomel reference electrode (0.24 V, vs. SHE) located 1 cm away from the working electrode [13]. CV was performed at a range of − 1.2 ∼ 1.4 V with a scan rate of 0.1 mV/s, which instead of a rapid scan rate (e.g., 1.0 mV/s) has been proven to guarantee a sufficiently long time for the system to reach steady conditions [33]. EIS was conducted under the electrode OCCs, over a frequency range of 100 kHz – 10 mHz with a sinusoidal perturbation of 10 mV amplitude. The equivalent circuit and detailed value of different resistances were obtained through Zsimpwin software.

Photoluminescence measurements were examined by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. UV–vis DRS spectra of the semiconductor photocatalysts were recorded with a Lambda 750 S UV–vis spectrophotometer. Mott-Schottky curves were measured in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte using the same potentiostat (VSP, BioLogic, France) with a potential range of –1.4 ∼ 1.4 V vs. SHE at a frequency of 5000 Hz and a sinusoidal modulation of 10 mV under darkness conditions [3,21].

The morphology observation and the elemental composition of the graphite felt cathodes covered by the CDs/g-C3N4, the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 or the sole metal oxides were examined using a SEM (QUANTA450, FEI company, USA) equipped with an EDS (X-MAX, Oxford Instruments, UK), and a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA) whereas the chemical components and binding energy change were studied by XPS (ESCALAB250, Thermo, USA).

Hydrogen production (m3/m3/d) was calculated as previously described [6,35]. Circuital current was normalized to the projected surface area of the cathode, allowing the comparison of the results to literature studies on photo-assisted MESs [38].

Anodic coulombic efficiency (CE an, %), cathodic coulombic efficiency (CE ca, %), and total coulombic efficiency (CE, %) were calculated according to Eqs. S1 – 3. Energy efficiency for either heavy metal removal (η metal, %) or H2 evolution (η H2, %) and relative with electrical input were calculated according to Eqs. S4 – 5 [13,22,23,30] whereas the solar-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency (η L, %) was calculated according to Eq. S6. [28]. Energy consumption (kWh/m3) was based on the difference between electrical energy consumed under darkness conditions and hydrogen equivalent energy (Eq. S7) [28].
One-way ANOVA in SPSS 19.0 was used to analyze the differences among the data, shown as a significance level of P < 0.05. Duplicate data were averagely shown.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimum of CDs and g-C3N4 ratios, and loading amounts
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of CDs and g-C3N4 catalysts ratios (Fig. S1) and loading amounts (Fig. S2) were explored to determine optimal system performance. A dosage of carbon quantum dots at 50 μL (Fig. S1), and a cathode photocatalyst loading amount of 1.44 mg/cm2 (Fig. S2) were thus selected to achieve the highest photo-response current (Fig. S1A; Fig. S2A), recalcitrant organics mineralization (Fig. S1B; Fig. S2B), hydrogen production (Fig. S1C; Fig. S2C), and circuital current (Fig. S1D; Fig. S2D).

A total 10-cycle operation always exhibited efficient recalcitrant organics mineralization (68.7%, 35.5 mg/L/h), heavy metals removal (Cu(II): 96.6%, 7.4 mg/L/h; Ni(II): 95.1%, 3.5 mg/L/h; Zn(II): 65.0%, 0.5 mg/L/h) ( Fig. 1 A) and H2 production (0.0662 m3/m3/d) (Fig. 1B) at a circuital current (4.99 A/m2) (Fig. 1C) in the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes of MECs, allowing the effluent (pH: 7.9 – 9.9, conductivity: 1.6 – 1.8; Fig. S3) meeting the China national wastewater discharge standards (GB39731–2020). The recalcitrant organics mineralization of 1.4 – 4.6 folds of the controls with g-C3N4, graphite felt, no light or OCCs (Fig. 1D), and the H2 production of 2.2 – 3.4 times of the g-C3N4, graphite felt, or no light controls (Fig. 1G) along with the circuital current (Fig. 1H), reflected the combined promotive effects of CDs, light irradiation and circuital current on both recalcitrant organics mineralization and H2 production in the MECs. The significant low recalcitrant organics mineralization and heavy metal removal along with no any H2 production in the OCCs (Fig. 1D and G), confirmed the tremendous importance of circuital current for the ETW treatment and H2 production using the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode MECs.

The presence of CDs increased circuital current, compared to the significant low values in the no light controls (Fig. 1H), indicating the critical role of CDs in separation and transformation of the electron-hole pairs for recalcitrant organics mineralization and H2 production instead of heavy metal removal. Moreover, CDs/g-C3N4 and the g-C3N4 controls always exhibited similar heavy metal removal at operational 2 h, 3 h or 4 h (Fig. 1E and F), further demonstrating negligible contribution of photocatalytic CDs to heavy metal removal, and thus the electrochemical removal of heavy metals, similar to other WO3/MoO3/g-C3N4 semiconductors [30]. These recalcitrant organics mineralization of 2.9 – 37 folds, heavy metals removal of 5.2 – 28 times, and H2 production of 1.6 – 8.0 folds of the WO3/MoO3/g-C3N4 photocathodes or graphite felt cathodes without illumination ([14]; 2023; [30]), demonstrated the appreciable CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes for efficient ETW treatment with simultaneous H2 production.

The high circuital current under light irradiation (Fig. 1H), consistent with high CE an, CE ca and CE (Table S2), implied the favorable light irradiation for a larger fraction of the electrons released by anodic organics oxidation and channeled into the cathodes concomitant with more proportional electrons utilized for H2 production and recovery of heavy metals in the ETW. The appreciable higher η H2 of 115 ± 2% than 100% indicated a positive energy feedback in the ETW treatment, consistent with other photocathodes for H2 production [28,44], in addition to the higher η L (0.5 ± 0.0%) than the MoS2-coated CuO photocathode MECs (0.36%) at similar applied voltages [18]. These along with the low energy consumption (0.10 kWh/m3) (Table S2) in concert gave a technology advantage over the controls (no CDs and/or g-C3N4: 0.14 – 0.27 kWh/m3), the conventional anaerobic or electrochemical systems (2 – 8 kWh/m3) [2,14] or the WO3/MoO3/g-C3N4 photocathodes (0.08 – 0.15 kWh/m3) [30] for the ETW treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ deposited heavy metals improved system performance
ce:section.ce:para: The progressively in-situ deposited metal oxides after a 5-cycle operation and followed by simple calcinations, further achieved the maximal enhanced recalcitrant organics mineralization (81.8 ± 1.3%, 42.2 ± 0.7 mg/L/h, p: 0.027) ( Fig. 2A) and high H2 production (0.1138 ± 0.0069 m3/m3/d, p: 0.036) (Fig. 2B) along with the high photo response current (Fig. 2C) than the freshly prepared CDs/g-C3N4 cathodes. This together with the absence of H2 in OCCs (Fig. 2E) excluded the appreciable H2 production via photocatalytic processes. With regard to heavy metal removal, operation times of 3 h or 4 h achieved similar performance (Cu(II): 96.7%, 9.9 mg/L/h,; Ni(II): 96.6%, 4.7 mg/L/h; Zn(II): 67.1%, 0.7 mg/L/h) (Fig. 2G), confirming the sufficient 3 h for efficient heavy metal removal. This along with the enhanced H2 production (Fig. 2E) and the higher circuital current (Fig. 2F), demonstrated the metal oxides had also acted as redox mediators for offering additional electrochemical processes for removal of heavy metals in the ETW with simultaneous more H2 production in the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes [20,39,40]. A prolonged 7-cycle operation might have led to overloading of the imported heavy metals and thus deactivated the activated molecules by collision with ground state molecules [1].

The recalcitrant organics mineralization was 4.5-fold of the OCCs and 3.1-time of the no light controls (Fig. 2D), whereas the H2 production (Fig. 2E) and the heavy metal removal (Fig. 2G − I) were 1.5-fold and 1.2 − 2.2 times of the CD/g-C3N4, and 3.32-fold and 1.2 – 2.4 times of the g-C3N4, respectively, reflecting the combined effect of light irradiation and circuital current on the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes for efficient ETW with simultaneous more H2 production. The higher circuital current exerted a higher driving force for extracting more electrons from anodic acetate and thus the higher CE an (Table S2), supported by other photo-bioelectrochemical systems [21,3,44]. The more H2 production relative to the less increase in circuital current explained the higher η H2 (p: 0.001) and CE ca (p: 0.001) (Eqs. 2 and 5) (Table S2). Collectively, these results demonstrated the extraordinary metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 for further enhanced ETW treatment with simultaneous more H2 production.

CV results demonstrated the importation of the metal oxides as catalysts toward both oxidation of recalcitrant organics and reduction of the heavy metals, shown as the shift of the organics oxidative peak potentials to less positive (0.57 V vs. 0.65 V) and the heavy metals reductive peak potentials to less negative (Cu(II): − 0.07 V vs. − 0.21 V; Ni(II): − 0.29 V vs. − 0.33 V; Zn(II): − 0.43 V vs. − 0.49 V) (Fig. 2J; Table S3). Accordingly, the invariable higher reductive peak currents (Ni(II): 1.35 mA vs. 1.25 mA; Zn(II): 1.44 mA vs. 1.31 mA; H+: 1.96 mA vs. 1.31 mA) or oxidative peak currents (1.22 mA vs. 1.17 mA) of the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 than the CDs/g-C3N4, suggested varying degrees of the reductive or oxidative dynamic mass transfer improvements [12,29]. The less positive oxidative onset potentials (0.05 V vs. 0.07 V), along with the more positive reductive onset potentials (Cu(II): 0.57 V vs. 0.51 V, Ni(II): − 0.19 V vs. − 0.23 V, Zn(II): − 0.38 V vs. − 0.41 V) (Fig. 2J; Table S3), implied a decrease in the corresponding thermodynamic overall free energy of the electron transfer reactions [12,29]. These results along with the other controls of darkness, g-C3N4 or sole graphite felt reflected the roles of metal oxides, CDs, g-C3N4 and light irradiation in the photo-electrochemical processes for mineralization of recalcitrant organics and removal of heavy metals in the ETW with simultaneous H2 production, consistent with the kinetics results in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2A – I.

The EIS spectra (equivalent circuits, Fig. S5) demonstrated a significant decrease in diffusion resistance (R dif, 102.1 Ω vs. 208.9 Ω) and charge transfer resistance (R ct, 24.1 Ω vs. 33.8 Ω) with a slight increase in solution and electrode resistance (R s, 27.9 Ω vs. 25.3 Ω) for the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 and relative to the CDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 2K; Table S4). The increase in R s suggested some degrees of mass transfer limitation whereas the sharp decrease in R dif and R ct implied the higher proportions of potential active sites on the CDs/g-C3N4 surface created by the importation of the metal oxides, which favored solution ions diffusion and charge transfer reactions. In addition, the absence of light irradiation invariably increased R dif (261.4 Ω vs. 102.1 Ω) and R ct (53.9 Ω vs. 24.1 Ω) with negligible effect on R s (27.4 Ω vs. 27.9 Ω), whereas the value of R dif was always dominant over the R s and the R ct regardless of the presence of light irradiation, metal oxides, CDs, or both CD and g-C3N4. Collectively, these results highlighted the roles of metal oxides, light irradiation, CDs, and both CDs and g-C3N4 in the activation of metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid photocatalyst, which in turn mainly reduced the diffusion resistance to electron transfer favoring the mineralization of recalcitrant organics and removal of heavy metals in the ETW with simultaneous H2 production (Fig. 1; Fig. 2A − I).

More red-shift in the absorption edges of the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 (475 nm) than the CDs/g-C3N4 (464 nm) and the g-C3N4 (444 nm) at a wide range of 350 – 750 nm in UV–vis DRS spectra ( Fig. 3A), along with the intrinsic narrower energy band gaps (E g) of 2.61 eV (metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4) than 2.67 eV (CDs/g-C3N4) and 2.79 eV (g-C3N4) estimated using Tauc’s equation [46], in concert indicated the utilizing of higher portions of the visible light spectrum for enhanced system performance (Fig. 1; Fig. 2A − I). The positive slopes of the straight sections in the Mott-Schottky plots of metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 3B), CDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 3C) and g-C3N4 (Fig. 3D) confirmed the n-type semiconducting nature of these components [1]. The CB potentials (E CB), the VB potentials (E VB) and the charge carrier densities (metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4: −1.26 eV, 1.05 eV, 9.96 × 1017 cm−3, Fig. 3B; CDs/g-C3N4: −0.95 eV, 1.42 eV, 8.71 × 1017 cm−3, Fig. 3C; g-C3N4: −1.14 eV, 1.35 eV, 5.36 × 1017 cm−3, Fig. 3D, respectively), in concert demonstrated the change of energy-level configuration of either CDs/g-C3N4 due to the importation of metals oxides, or the g-C3N4 due to the importation of CDs, as well as the efficient electron-hole of the semiconductor in order of metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 > CD/g-C3N4 > g-C3N4. In addition, the sharply weaker maximum emission intensity of the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 than those of CDs/g-C3N4 or g-C3N4 at the same 435 nm in the photoluminescence spectra (Fig. 3E), along with the higher photocurrent (7.66 μA) than the controls (CDs/g-C3N4: 6.67 μA; g-C3N4: 1.24 μA) (Fig. 3F), indicated the appreciable interactions, lower recombination rates of the photo-generated carriers, and highly efficient charge carrier separation and transport between metal oxides and CDs/g-C3N4, or CDs and g-C3N4. Collectively, these results demonstrated the extraordinary higher photocatalytic activities of the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 than the CDs/g-C3N4, both of which were higher than the g-C3N4, explaining the efficient ETW treatment with simultaneous more H2 production (Fig. 1; Fig. 2A − I).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of the cathodes, product elemental confirmation and XPS survey
ce:section.ce:para: Small bright yellow particles with an overall dark appearance were observed for the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 obtained after a 5-cycle operation of the CDs/g-C3N4 in the ETW catholyte, and followed by simple calcinations ( Fig. 4A), compared to the light-yellow single CDs/g-C3N4 powders uniformly covered on the graphite felt (Fig. 4D) and the black-and-yellow metal oxides powders (Fig. 4G). SEM observed nano-metric scale particles of metal oxides uniformly covered on the surface of the irregular aggregates of the lamellar CDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 4B), compared to the controls of either lamellar CDs/g-C3N4 with small and hardly observable CDs (Fig. 4E) or the amorphous metal oxides (Fig. 4H). EDS spectra (Fig. 4C, F and I; Table S5) reflected the chemical composition of these different cathodes. The elemental information of the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 was also recorded by the elemental mapping (Fig. 4J – O), where C, N, O, Cu, Ni and Zn elements were invariably detected. These results in concert demonstrated the successful simultaneous deposits of multiple metals (Cu, Ni and Zn) in the ETW on the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes of MECs.

The HRTEM image shows the simultaneous presence of metal oxides and the CDs on the graphite felt with the lattice stripes of 0.24 nm and 0.22 nm that correspond to the metal oxides, and CDs, respectively ( Fig. 5A and B), compared to the lots of CDs decorated on the surface of g-C3N4 graphite felt (Fig. 5C and D), and the single dispersed CDs in solution (Fig. 5E and F). This evidences the successful in-situ deposited fabrication of metal oxides/CDs on the g-C3N4, consistent with the reports in conventional photocatalytic processes [11,24,25,50]. The obvious interface between metal oxides and g-C3N4 (Fig. 5A and B) indicates the physical contact and thus a combination of the two as well as the interaction between metal oxides and g-C3N4 to form the semiconductor.

The metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 XPS spectra contained the elements of C, N, O, Cu, Ni and Zn in comparison of C, N and O with CDs/g-C3N4 ( Fig. 6A − F). The none shift of the characteristic peak at 284.8 eV with a small shift (288.2 eV vs. 288.3 eV) indexed to C – C and sp2-hybridized carbon within the triazine rings (N − CN) in the C 1 s XPS (Fig. 6A), indicated the negligible effect of the metal oxides on the original structure of CDs/g-C3N4 [31]. Whereas, the characteristic peak at 286.5 eV corresponding to C−O species in the C 1 s XPS (Fig. 6A) along with the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 6B) deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.3 eV (sp2-bonded N, C−N = C in triazine rings), 400.1 eV (tertiary N in N − (C)3) and 404.3 eV (amino groups N − H), invariably exhibited shift after the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 modification [9,24].

The higher binding energies of the Cu 2p (935.0 eV vs. 933.5 eV, 955.1 eV vs. 953.5 eV; satellite peaks: 941.6 eV vs. 941.0 eV, 944.3 eV vs. 943.5 eV, 963.3 eV vs. 962.1 eV) (Fig. 6C), along with the Ni 2p (Ni 2p3/2: 855.5 eV vs. 855.2 eV, Ni 2p1/2: 873.3 eV vs. 873.0 eV; satellites peaks: 861.4 eV vs. 861.0 eV, 879.6 eV vs. 879.1 eV) (Fig. 6D), confirmed the imported Cu and Ni elements from the ETW, and their chemical interactions with the CDs/g-C3N4 for efficient interfacial charge transfer [25,50]. Accordingly, the slight lower Zn 2p3/2 (1021.1 eV vs. 1021.4 eV) and Zn 2p5/2 (1044.2 eV vs. 1044.5 eV) in the Zn 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 6E), was explained by the increased electron concentration and thus elevating the strong interaction between the ZnO/CDs/g-C3N4 and the CDs based on the interfacial charge transfer [11]. Collectively, these results along with the increased binding energies of O 1 s (530.8 eV vs. 530.7 eV; satellite peaks: 531.7 eV vs. 531.4 eV; 532.6 eV vs. 532.1 eV) (Fig. 6F) [9,11], confirmed the simultaneously imported Cu, Ni and Zn oxides, and their chemical interactions with the CDs/g-C3N4 for efficient interfacial charge transfer, and thus enhanced ETW treatment with simultaneous more H2 production (Fig. 1; Fig. 2A − I).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible pathways of recalcitrant organic mineralization
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-electrochemically generated O2 - (38.2 ± 1.0%), similar to the ˙OH (38.8 ± 1.1%, p: 0.72), mainly dominated the mineralization of recalcitrant organics, as confirmed by p-benzoquinone and isopropanol scavengers, respectively compared to the controls of no scavenger addition (81.8 ± 1.3%, p: 0.001 – 0.002) ( Fig. 7A). The influences of O2 - and ˙OH were more significant than the holes (50.0 ± 1.2%, p: 0.02 – 0.03; scavenger: ammonium oxalate) and the H2O2 (62.2 ± 1.8%, p: 0.01; scavenger: Fe(II)-EDTA), implying the possible pathways of recalcitrant organics mineralization in the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes through the oxidative active species as the order of O2 - ≈ ˙OH > holes > H2O2.

In the sole CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes with low mineralization of recalcitrant organics (67.9 ± 2.0%), contribution by these active species varying degrees decreased, shown as O2 - (42.0 ± 0.9%) ≈ ˙OH (44.5 ± 0.9%) > holes (56.4 ± 0.7%) ≈ H2O2 (63.3 ± 3.1%) (Fig. 7B). In the controls of individual circuital current (Fig. 7C) or sole light irradiation (Fig. 7D), the active species functioned as O2 - > ˙OH ≈ H2O2 > holes (total: 26.1 ± 3.9%) and O2 - > ˙OH ≈ holes > H2O2 (total: 18.0 ± 1.5%), respectively (Fig. 7C and D). These results in concert demonstrated the altering proportion of oxidative active species (O2 -, ˙OH, holes and H2O2) via interaction between metal oxides and CDs/g-C3N4, as well as the roles of light irradiation and circuital current in the photoelectrochemical processes for efficient mineralization of recalcitrant organics in the ETW. In summary, the possible pathways of recalcitrant organics mineralization along with the chemical active species transformation processes in the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes were briefly outlined (Fig. 7E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: System stability and electrode morphological observation
ce:section.ce:para: The metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode always achieved efficient recalcitrant organics mineralization (79.7 – 82.4%, p: 0.41) and heavy metals removal (Cu(II): 98.1 – 99.0%, p: 0.46; Ni(II): 96.2 – 97.5%, p: 0.43; Zn(II): 60.1 – 69.7%, p: 0.21) ( Fig. 8A) with appreciable high H2 production (0.098 – 0.112 m3/m3/d, p: 0.12) (Fig. 8B) at stable circuital currents (5.58 – 5.77 A/m2, p: 0.71) (Fig. 8C) during the 10 cycles operation. This allowed the stable effluent pHs at 9.59 – 9.97 and solution conductivities at 1.79 – 1.90 mS/cm (Fig. S5).

This stability of metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode over time was evidenced by the negligible changes of the organics oxidative peak potentials, the very small shifts of reductive peak potentials of both the heavy metals and the H+, as well as the slight more negative shift of the heavy metals and the H+ reductive onset potentials (Fig. S6A; Table S3). This along with the dominant R dif with negligible changes (102 Ω vs. 107 Ω) and the R s (27.6 Ω vs. 29.8 Ω) during these 10 cycles operation (Fig. S6B; Table S4), was consistent with the kinetics results (Fig. 8A – C), despite the somewhat decrease in heavy metals reductive peak currents and H+ reductive peak current as well as the increase in R ct (Fig. S6; Table S3; Table S4).

This prolonged 10-cycle operation achieved distribution of larger irregular-shaped agglomerates for the metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 cathode (Fig. 8D and E), consistently accompanied with the appreciable stronger Cu, Ni and Zn signals (Fig. 8F; Table S5), compared to those freshly prepared metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 4A − C). This along with the EDS elemental mappings, shown as more uniform distribution of Cu (Fig. 8J), Ni (Fig. 8K) and Zn (Fig. 8L) elements after the 10-cycle operation than the freshly prepared metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 4M − O), as well as similar C, N and O elements (Fig. 8G – I; Fig. 4J − L), evidently illustrated the progressively accumulated Cu, Ni and Zn over time, despite that SEM alone cannot be used to exactly quantify the heavy metals accumulation on the cathode.

The extraordinary CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode was for the first time employed for efficient ETW treatment with simultaneous H2 production, allowing the effluent meeting national discharge standards. The in-situ deposited heavy metals, formed as metal oxides/CDs/g-C3N4 after simple calcinations further took advantage of the sunlight and enhanced the charge transfer and separation efficiency, exhibiting a redshift of absorption edge, modifying the energy-level configuration and narrowing the bandgap of CDs/g-C3N4, and preventing the recombination of photoexcited carriers. As a result, the proportions of reactive oxidative species including O2 -, ˙OH, holes and H2O2 were altered via interaction between metal oxides and CDs/g-C3N4 in the photoelectrochemical processes, which in turn promoted mineralization of recalcitrant organics. In addition, the metal oxides acted as redox mediators for offering additional electrochemical removal of heavy metals in the ETW and H2 evolution on the CDs/g-C3N4.

CDs and g-C3N4 are cost-effective heterogeneous nanocomposites that can be easily prepared [1,40], whereas the poisonous ETW contains multiple heavy metals and recalcitrant organics [2,48]. The design of metal oxide/CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode via in-situ deposited heavy metals for further efficient ETW treatment with simultaneous more H2 production, has thus offered a new alternative protocol. This not only brings a step forward practical application of the sustainable, cost-effective and efficient CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode MECs, but also contributes to sustainable development of the electronic and semiconducting industry as very recently advocated by Zuo et al. [49] and Xu et al. [44]. These win-win benefits thus broaden the application horizon of the unique CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode MECs as an attractive and environmental desirable process for the efficient ETW treatment with simultaneous more H2 production, particularly in conjunction with the concomitant in-situ recovery and utilization of value-added heavy metals from the wastewaters e.g., ETW.

Since many metal oxides are found to be successfully adopted and coupled with CDs/g-C3N4 for photocatalytic degradation of many individual organics and/or H2 production [1,40,41], any wastewaters containing these similar metal ions and organics could be ideally developed and in-situ utilized in the hybrid nanostructures of the CDs/g-C3N4 system with concurrent treatment of wastewaters. In view of this point, this study opens up the new feasibility of the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode as a powerful and universal suitable process for treatment of many complex industrial wastewaters containing multiple heavy metals and recalcitrant organics with simultaneous production of renewable energy (e.g., H2) [44,49]. However, in terms of synthesis of CD-based photo-electrocatalysts generally required precise chemical compositions [10,40,41,5], the complexity of actual industrial wastewaters even with small amounts of other components (e.g., S and N) may varying degrees affect the composition, structure and performance of the in-situ produced photoelectrochemical catalysts. To what extent of this influence should be further quantified and extensively assessed based on treatment of a series of typical complex industrial wastewaters using this CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode.

Besides photocathode materials, the cathode architecture with a designed three-phase interface, preferably through an air-breathing gas diffusion electrode with a large geometrical area using a roll-to-roll fabrication technique [27], might allow the natural diffusion of more oxygen from the ambient air and thus for producing sufficient oxidative active species of O2 - through photocatalytic process and H2O2 via electrocatalytic process [36,37] for more efficient ETW treatment. However, light transmittance across the air-breathing gas diffusion electrode in most cases made of carbon-based materials (e.g., carbon cloth), might face challenges. This along with the recent introduced light transmittance for efficient light management in microbial photo-electrosynthesis systems [23], might require the ingenious design of transparent air-breathing gas diffusion photocathode with three-phase interface in the CDs/g-C3N4.

Practical implementation of the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode for the complex real ETW treatment requires further scale-up investigations, to evaluate the long-term operation and stability of the system over feeds with fluctuating characteristics [17,38,45]. In addition, the photo-assisted MECs herein were operated in the commonly used fed-batch mode, due to its standardized and practical setup for bench-scale experiments [7,8]. Considering the impractical batch operation and the most likely continuous operation for real-life conditions wastewater treatment, further investigations on continuously operated scale-up CDs/g-C3N4 photocathode MECs for the ETW treatment are warranted.

In this study, CDs/g-C3N4 semiconducting photocathodes were for the first time employed to achieve efficient ETW treatment (recalcitrant organics mineralization: 35.5 mg/L/h; heavy metal removal: Cu(II): 7.4 mg/L/h, Ni(II): 3.5 mg/L/h, Zn(II): 0.5 mg/L/h) with simultaneous H2 production (0.0662 m3/m3/d) in the MECs, allowing the effluent meeting national discharge standards. The progressively in-situ deposited heavy metals on the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes after a 5-cycle operation and followed by simple calcinations, further enhanced ETW treatment (recalcitrant organics mineralization: 42.2 mg/L/h; heavy metals removal: Cu(II): 9.9 mg/L/h, Ni(II): 4.7 mg/L/h, Zn(II): 0.7 mg/L/h) and H2 production (0.1138 m3/m3/d). The importation of metal oxides on the CDs/g-C3N4 exhibited a redshift of absorption edge, modifying the energy-level configuration and narrowing the bandgap of CDs/g-C3N4, and preventing the recombination of photoexcited carriers. As a result, the proportion of reactive oxidative species (O2 -, ˙OH, holes and H2O2) was altered via interaction between the metal oxides and the CDs/g-C3N4, and thus promoted mineralization of recalcitrant organics in the ETW. Meanwhile, the metal oxides acted as redox mediators for offering additional electrochemical removal of heavy metals in the same ETW concomitant with more H2 production. This study provides a new feasible protocol for achieving efficient actual ETW treatment with simultaneous H2 production, and gives a comprehensive appreciation of the effect of in-situ deposited heavy metals on the CDs/g-C3N4 photocathodes, which has a profound effect on the subsequent ETW treatment with simultaneous H2 production.

Jiahua Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Qiang Wang: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Huilin Wan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Yong Shi: Methodology, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Liping Huang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.
